summary: Patrick McKenzie and Nathan Barry discuss the strategy of 'teaching as marketing' — building an audience and credibility by publicly sharing expertise, then monetizing through productized knowledge (books, courses, email lists). They cover how Nathan launched multiple successful books without being 'Internet famous,' how tiered pricing packages can double or triple revenue compared to single-price offerings, and why email lists are the most valuable business asset for independent creators. The conversation emphasizes that you don't need celebrity status to build authority — consistent teaching creates perceived expertise that translates directly into sales.
key_points: Teaching publicly builds authority and audience simultaneously — the difference between two equally skilled people is that one shared knowledge while the other kept it private
Tiered pricing packages (e.g., $39/$99/$249) let customers self-segment by value received, consistently doubling or tripling revenue versus a single price point
Email lists are the most valuable business asset for independent creators — they function as 'recurring revenue for traffic' and provide a reliable, owned distribution channel

Critics who say 'nobody will pay for information' are almost never the target buyers — pricing objections from $30/hour freelancers are irrelevant when selling to businesses where the product cost rounds to zero
You don't get real market validation feedback unless you actually ask for money — verbal interest and willingness to pay are completely different things
money_quotes: The Internet has a short temper and a long memory.
Anyone who has employees, and accordingly must pay salaries every two weeks regardless of what they are doing, is literally incapable of finding things for free on the Internet.

We are literally incapable of tracking a number this low on our systems. The expected value of a single lead exceeds the price of this course by several times. That is making it difficult for me to convince my boss to buy it.
This is like the vegetarians complaining about the prices from the hot dog vendors.

There exists that psychograph of people who just are fundamentally unhappy with the notion that products sell. They're empirically not that good at predicting that because all products ever have sold.
Every time I've seen multiple packages in use, both when I do it and when other people do it, it consistently doubles revenue if not triples revenue.
You don't get real feedback unless you ask for money.
Pricing is generally not something you should ask customers for. That's something you should announce to them and get the up-vote or down-vote on it.
key_insight: Building an audience through consistent public teaching creates authority and trust that converts directly into product sales, and smart packaging with tiered pricing lets customers self-segment so you capture value proportional to what each buyer receives.
